tasks.title: Setup
tasks.description: Review existing contracts directory structure and understand the UserTransactions.sol contract pattern. Verify Remix IDE deployment approach for Sepolia testnet.
tasks.details:
The project already has a contracts/ directory at the root level (E:\FYP\w3uptime\contracts\) containing UserTransactions.sol, which demonstrates the existing contract patterns.

UserTransactions.sol Analysis:
- Uses Solidity ^0.8.20 (same version needed for W3Governance)
- Imports OpenZeppelin contracts: Ownable, ReentrancyGuard, Pausable, ECDSA, MessageHashUtils
- Implements signature verification pattern using ECDSA.recover() and MessageHashUtils.toEthSignedMessageHash()
- Uses custom errors (InvalidAmount, InsufficientContractBalance, etc.) instead of require statements
- Follows modern Solidity patterns with events for all state changes
- Includes owner-only administrative functions with proper access control

Key Files to Review:
- contracts/UserTransactions.sol:1-173 - Primary reference for contract structure
- Reference in PRD: scripts/prd.txt:192-238 describes expected contracts/ directory structure for W3Governance.sol

Deployment Approach:
- Remix IDE will be used for deployment to Sepolia (no Hardhat setup needed)
- Contract artifacts (ABI, address) will need to be manually extracted from Remix
- Similar to UserTransactions.sol pattern, W3Governance.sol should use OpenZeppelin ^0.8.20 imports

Review Tasks:
1. Document the signature verification pattern used in UserTransactions.sol (lines 102-106, 122-133)
2. Understand the custom error pattern (lines 51-59) for gas efficiency
3. Note the event emission pattern (lines 19-48) for transparency
4. Review constructor pattern (lines 61-73) with validation
5. Understand Ownable pattern from OpenZeppelin (inherited on line 9)
6. Plan W3Governance.sol to follow similar patterns: EIP-712 signatures, custom errors, comprehensive events

tasks.title: Create Shared Governance Types
tasks.description: Define shared TypeScript types for governance system in packages/common
tasks.details:
Create packages/common/governance-types.ts file and add export to packages/common/package.json. This file will define shared types for the on-chain governance system where users vote directly via blockchain transactions (no gasless signatures).

The project uses ethers.js v6 (^6.15.0 in frontend, ^6.13.4 in validator) and follows existing patterns in packages/common/contract.ts and packages/common/types.ts for type definitions and helper functions.

Create packages/common/governance-types.ts with the following:

1. OnChainStatus enum:
```typescript
export enum OnChainStatus {
  DRAFT = 'DRAFT',                    // Not yet submitted to blockchain
  PENDING_ONCHAIN = 'PENDING_ONCHAIN', // Transaction submitted, awaiting confirmation
  ACTIVE = 'ACTIVE',                   // On-chain and accepting votes
  PASSED = 'PASSED',                   // Voting ended, proposal passed
  FAILED = 'FAILED'                    // Voting ended, proposal failed
}
```

2. VoteType enum:
```typescript
export enum VoteType {
  UPVOTE = 'UPVOTE',
  DOWNVOTE = 'DOWNVOTE'
}
```

3. OnChainProposal interface matching the governance smart contract struct:
```typescript
export interface OnChainProposal {
  id: number;                    // On-chain proposal ID
  proposer: string;              // Ethereum address of proposer
  contentHash: string;           // IPFS hash or content identifier
  createdAt: bigint;             // Unix timestamp from blockchain
  votingEndsAt: bigint;          // Unix timestamp when voting closes
  upvotes: bigint;               // Total upvote count
  downvotes: bigint;             // Total downvote count
  finalized: boolean;            // Whether voting has been finalized
  passed: boolean;               // Whether proposal passed (only valid if finalized)
}
```

4. VoteCastEvent interface for tracking individual votes:
```typescript
export interface VoteCastEvent {
  proposalId: number;            // On-chain proposal ID
  voter: string;                 // Ethereum address of voter
  support: boolean;              // true = upvote, false = downvote
  txHash: string;                // Transaction hash
  blockNumber: number;           // Block number where vote was recorded
  timestamp?: bigint;            // Optional: block timestamp
}
```

5. Helper function to convert contract response to OnChainProposal:
```typescript
export function convertContractToProposal(contractData: unknown): OnChainProposal {
  // Assumes contractData is tuple/array from contract call
  // Add proper type guards and validation
  // Convert contract response format to OnChainProposal interface
}
```

6. Helper function to convert VoteType to boolean for contract calls:
```typescript
export function voteTypeToSupport(voteType: VoteType): boolean {
  return voteType === VoteType.UPVOTE;
}
```

7. Helper function to convert contract boolean to VoteType:
```typescript
export function supportToVoteType(support: boolean): VoteType {
  return support ? VoteType.UPVOTE : VoteType.DOWNVOTE;
}
```

Update packages/common/package.json to add export:
```json
"./governance-types": "./governance-types.ts"
```

Follow the pattern established in packages/common/contract.ts for helper functions and type definitions. Add comprehensive JSDoc comments explaining each type and its purpose in the on-chain governance system.

NO EIP-712 types, NO signature-related code, NO nonces, NO VOTE_DOMAIN, NO VOTE_TYPES - this is purely for direct on-chain voting via MetaMask transactions.

tasks.title: Develop W3Governance Smart Contract
tasks.description: Create W3Governance Solidity contract with direct on-chain voting
tasks.details:
Create contracts/W3Governance.sol using Solidity ^0.8.20, following the same patterns as UserTransactions.sol (contracts/UserTransactions.sol:1-173).

Import OpenZeppelin contracts (same as UserTransactions.sol):
- @openzeppelin/contracts/access/Ownable.sol
- @openzeppelin/contracts/security/ReentrancyGuard.sol
- @openzeppelin/contracts/security/Pausable.sol

State variables:
- mapping(uint256 => Proposal) public proposals
- mapping(uint256 => mapping(address => uint8)) public votes // 0=not voted, 1=upvote, 2=downvote
- uint256 public proposalCount

Proposal struct:
```solidity
struct Proposal {
  uint256 id;
  address proposer;
  bytes32 contentHash;
  uint256 createdAt;
  uint256 votingEndsAt;
  uint256 upvotes;
  uint256 downvotes;
  bool finalized;
  bool passed;
}
```

Functions:
- createProposal(bytes32 contentHash, uint256 votingDuration) external returns (uint256) - creates new proposal, increments proposalCount, emits ProposalCreated
- vote(uint256 proposalId, bool support) external - users call directly on-chain, pays own gas, records vote in mapping, updates upvotes/downvotes counters, emits VoteCast. Validates: proposal exists, voting period not ended, user hasn't already voted
- finalizeProposal(uint256 proposalId) external - anyone can call after votingEndsAt, tallies final counts from Proposal struct, determines pass/fail (2/3 majority required), marks finalized=true, emits ProposalFinalized
- getProposal(uint256 proposalId) external view returns (Proposal memory) - returns proposal details
- getVote(uint256 proposalId, address voter) external view returns (bool hasVoted, bool support) - checks votes mapping, returns whether voter voted and their vote direction

Events:
- ProposalCreated(uint256 indexed proposalId, address indexed proposer, bytes32 contentHash, uint256 votingEndsAt)
- VoteCast(uint256 indexed proposalId, address indexed voter, bool support)
- ProposalFinalized(uint256 indexed proposalId, uint256 upvotes, uint256 downvotes, bool passed)

Security patterns (follow UserTransactions.sol):
- Custom errors instead of require statements (e.g., InvalidProposalId(), VotingEnded(), AlreadyVoted(), VotingNotEnded(), AlreadyFinalized(), InvalidVotingDuration())
- ReentrancyGuard on vote() and finalizeProposal() to prevent reentrancy attacks
- onlyOwner for pause/unpause functions inherited from Pausable
- whenNotPaused modifier on vote() to allow emergency pause of voting
- Input validation: proposalId exists, voting period validation, prevent double voting, prevent finalization before voting ends, prevent duplicate finalization

Deployment: Will be deployed manually using Remix IDE to Sepolia testnet (no Hardhat deployment scripts needed).
tasks.testStrategy: Manual testing in Remix IDE: (1) Deploy contract to Sepolia testnet via Remix. (2) Verify contract on Etherscan. (3) Test createProposal() emits ProposalCreated with correct contentHash and votingEndsAt timestamp. (4) Test vote() with support=true increments upvotes counter and emits VoteCast event. (5) Test vote() with support=false increments downvotes counter. (6) Test vote() prevents double voting from same address (reverts with AlreadyVoted error). (7) Test vote() reverts after votingEndsAt with VotingEnded error. (8) Test finalizeProposal() with 2/3+ majority (e.g., 10 upvotes, 4 downvotes) marks passed=true. (9) Test finalizeProposal() with <2/3 majority marks passed=false. (10) Test finalizeProposal() reverts before votingEndsAt with VotingNotEnded error. (11) Test finalizeProposal() prevents duplicate finalization with AlreadyFinalized error. (12) Test pause() stops voting temporarily. (13) Monitor gas costs for vote() and finalizeProposal() via Etherscan. (14) Test getVote() returns correct hasVoted and support values for voters.

tasks.title: Create Smart Contract Deployment Script
tasks.description: Deploy W3Governance contract to Sepolia testnet via Remix IDE and save deployment information
tasks.details:
Deploy W3Governance contract using Remix IDE workflow:

1. **Open Remix IDE**: Navigate to https://remix.ethereum.org in browser
2. **Import Contract Files**: 
   - Create new file `W3Governance.sol` in Remix workspace
   - Copy contract code from contracts/W3Governance.sol (to be created in Task 3)
   - Import any dependencies if needed
3. **Compile Contract**:
   - Select Solidity compiler version ^0.8.20 in Remix compiler tab
   - Enable optimization if desired (200 runs recommended)
   - Click 'Compile W3Governance.sol'
   - Verify no compilation errors
4. **Connect MetaMask to Sepolia**:
   - Install MetaMask browser extension if not installed
   - Switch MetaMask network to Sepolia Testnet
   - Ensure deployer wallet has sufficient Sepolia ETH (minimum 0.1 ETH recommended)
   - Get test ETH from Sepolia faucet if needed: https://sepoliafaucet.com
5. **Deploy Contract**:
   - Go to 'Deploy & Run Transactions' tab in Remix
   - Select 'Injected Provider - MetaMask' as environment
   - Verify connected account and network in Remix matches MetaMask
   - Enter constructor parameters if required by W3Governance contract
   - Click 'Deploy' button
   - Confirm transaction in MetaMask popup
   - Wait for deployment confirmation (2 blocks)
6. **Record Deployment Information**:
   - Copy deployed contract address from Remix console
   - Copy transaction hash from Remix console
   - Note block number and deployer address
   - Create file `deployments/sepolia-governance.json` with:
   ```json
   {
     "contractAddress": "0x...",
     "txHash": "0x...",
     "blockNumber": 123456,
     "deployer": "0x...",
     "timestamp": "2025-12-11T...",
     "network": "sepolia",
     "contractName": "W3Governance"
   }
   ```
7. **Update Environment Variables**:
   - Add `NEXT_PUBLIC_GOVERNANCE_CONTRACT_ADDRESS=0x...` to `.env` and `.env.example`
   - Update `packages/common/governance-contract.ts` to export governance contract address
8. **Verify Contract on Sepolia Etherscan**:
   - Navigate to https://sepolia.etherscan.io/address/[CONTRACT_ADDRESS]
   - Click 'Contract' tab, then 'Verify and Publish'
   - Select 'Solidity (Single File)' or 'Solidity (Standard JSON Input)'
   - Enter compiler version (0.8.20+), optimization settings
   - Paste contract source code
   - Submit for verification
   - Wait for verification confirmation

**Alternative Verification** (if Remix verification fails):
- Use Hardhat verify plugin: `npx hardhat verify --network sepolia [CONTRACT_ADDRESS] [CONSTRUCTOR_ARGS]`
- Requires ETHERSCAN_API_KEY in .env file

**Error Handling**:
- Insufficient funds: Get more Sepolia ETH from faucet
- Network issues: Check MetaMask connection, try different RPC endpoint
- Verification failures: Ensure exact compiler settings match, check constructor args
- Gas estimation failures: Increase gas limit manually in MetaMask

**Documentation**:
- Create `docs/deployment-guide.md` documenting the deployment process
- Include screenshots of successful deployment for reference
- Document any issues encountered and solutions
tasks.testStrategy:
Manual testing checklist:
1. **Pre-Deployment**: Verify contract compiles in Remix with no errors, check MetaMask connected to Sepolia, confirm sufficient test ETH balance
2. **Deployment**: Successfully deploy contract via Remix, receive transaction hash, deployment appears in MetaMask activity
3. **On-Chain Verification**: Contract address viewable on Sepolia Etherscan at https://sepolia.etherscan.io/address/[ADDRESS], transaction shows in 'Internal Txns' tab
4. **Contract Interaction**: In Remix 'Deployed Contracts' section, test calling read-only functions (e.g., getProposal(0) returns empty/default values), verify contract responds correctly
5. **File Creation**: Verify `deployments/sepolia-governance.json` exists with correct data format, environment variable added to `.env` and `.env.example`
6. **Source Verification**: Contract source code visible on Sepolia Etherscan 'Contract' tab (green checkmark), ABI visible and matches compiled output, 'Read Contract' and 'Write Contract' tabs functional on Etherscan
7. **Integration Test**: Import contract in `packages/common/governance-contract.ts`, create contract instance using deployed address, call contract functions from frontend code successfully

**Success Criteria**:
- Contract deployed to Sepolia with verified source code
- Deployment artifacts saved to `deployments/sepolia-governance.json`
- Environment variables updated with contract address
- Contract callable from Remix and Etherscan interfaces
- Documentation created for future deployments

tasks.title: Extend Prisma Schema for On-Chain Governance
tasks.description: Add on-chain tracking fields to Proposal model and create VoteCache model for caching blockchain votes
tasks.details:
Edit packages/db/prisma/schema.prisma (packages/db/prisma/schema.prisma:293-309). Extend existing Proposal model with NULLABLE fields (backward compatible):

// Add to Proposal model (after line 302)
onChainId           Int?              @unique
contentHash         String?
creationTxHash      String?           @unique
finalizationTxHash  String?           @unique
votingEndsAt        DateTime?
onChainStatus       OnChainStatus     @default(DRAFT)

// Add relation
voteCaches          VoteCache[]

Create new VoteCache model for caching on-chain votes:
model VoteCache {
  id                  String    @id @default(uuid())
  proposalId          String
  onChainProposalId   Int
  voterAddress        String
  voteType            VoteType  // UPVOTE or DOWNVOTE (existing enum at line 406)
  txHash              String    @unique
  blockNumber         Int
  createdAt           DateTime  @default(now())

  proposal            Proposal  @relation(fields: [proposalId], references: [id], onDelete: Cascade)

  @@unique([proposalId, voterAddress])
  @@index([proposalId])
  @@index([onChainProposalId])
  @@index([voterAddress])
  @@index([txHash])
}

Create OnChainStatus enum (add after VoteType enum around line 409):
enum OnChainStatus {
  DRAFT              // Not yet submitted on-chain
  PENDING_ONCHAIN    // Transaction submitted, awaiting confirmation
  ACTIVE             // On-chain and voting active
  PASSED             // Finalized and passed
  FAILED             // Finalized but failed
}

Add indexes to Proposal model for efficient queries:
@@index([onChainId])
@@index([onChainStatus])
@@index([votingEndsAt])

NOTE: This design uses DIRECT on-chain voting. VoteCache is ONLY for fast queries to cache blockchain data. NO SignedVote model, NO signatures, NO nonces - all votes are recorded on blockchain and VoteCache mirrors them for performance.

2. New VoteCache table with all specified fields and relations

4. Indexes on Proposal (onChainId, onChainStatus, votingEndsAt)
5. Indexes on VoteCache (proposalId, onChainProposalId, voterAddress, txHash)

Query proposal with include: { voteCaches: true }. Verify unique constraints work (duplicate voterAddress for same proposalId should fail). Run 'EXPLAIN ANALYZE' on PostgreSQL to verify indexes are created and used for queries filtering by onChainStatus and onChainProposalId.

tasks.title: Implement On-Chain Proposal Creation API
tasks.description: Enhance POST /api/proposals to support on-chain proposal creation with transaction handling
tasks.details:
Update apps/frontend/app/api/proposals/route.ts POST handler. Extend createProposalSchema:

const createProposalSchema = z.object({
  title: z.string().min(1),
  description: z.string().min(1),
  type: z.enum(['FEATURE_REQUEST', 'CHANGE_REQUEST']),
  tags: z.array(z.string()).optional().default([]),
  createOnChain: z.boolean().optional().default(false),
  txHash: z.string().optional(), // Transaction hash from user's wallet
});

Flow:
1. Validate request with reputation check (existing)
2. Generate contentHash using generateContentHash()
3. If createOnChain=true:
   a. Require txHash parameter
   b. Verify transaction exists and is confirmed on Sepolia using ethers.js provider
   c. Decode transaction logs to get ProposalCreated event
   d. Extract onChainId from event
   e. Calculate votingEndsAt (createdAt + 7 days)
   f. Set onChainStatus = ACTIVE
4. Create Proposal record with on-chain fields
5. Return proposal with on-chain metadata

Create helper function verifyProposalTransaction(txHash: string, contentHash: string, proposer: string): Promise<{ onChainId: number, blockNumber: number }>. Use AlchemyProvider or InfuraProvider for Sepolia. Handle errors: transaction not found, not confirmed, event not in logs, contentHash mismatch.

tasks.title: Create VoteCast Event Listener Service
tasks.description: Build service to sync on-chain votes to database cache
tasks.details:
Create a new governance event listener service following the existing blockchain-listener.ts pattern (apps/frontend/lib/blockchain-listener.ts:1-524).

**1. Create VoteCache Database Table**
Add to packages/db/prisma/schema.prisma:
```prisma
model VoteCache {
  id           String   @id @default(uuid())
  proposalId   Int
  voter        String   // Ethereum address
  support      Boolean  // true = upvote, false = downvote
  txHash       String   @unique
  blockNumber  Int
  timestamp    DateTime
  createdAt    DateTime @default(now())
  updatedAt    DateTime @updatedAt

  @@unique([proposalId, voter])
  @@index([proposalId])
  @@index([voter])
  @@index([blockNumber])
}
```
Run: `npx prisma migrate dev --name add_vote_cache`

**2. Create Governance Contract Configuration**
Create packages/common/governance-contract.ts:
```typescript
import { ethers } from 'ethers';

export const GOVERNANCE_CONTRACT_ADDRESS = process.env.NEXT_PUBLIC_GOVERNANCE_CONTRACT_ADDRESS || '0x...';

export const GOVERNANCE_ABI = [
  'event VoteCast(uint256 indexed proposalId, address indexed voter, bool support)',
  'function getProposal(uint256 proposalId) view returns (tuple(uint256 id, address proposer, bytes32 contentHash, uint256 createdAt, uint256 votingEndsAt, uint256 upvotes, uint256 downvotes, bool finalized, bool passed))',
] as const;

export function createGovernanceContractInstance(provider: ethers.Provider) {
  return new ethers.Contract(GOVERNANCE_CONTRACT_ADDRESS, GOVERNANCE_ABI, provider);
}
```

**3. Create Governance Listener Service**
Create apps/frontend/lib/governance-listener.ts based on blockchain-listener.ts pattern:

- Class GovernanceListener with similar structure to BlockchainListener
- Connect to ETHEREUM_RPC_URL provider
- Listen for VoteCast events from governance contract
- Implement handleVoteCastEvent() method
- Process past events on startup (last 50 blocks in chunks)
- Handle reconnection with exponential backoff (same as blockchain-listener.ts:445-460)
- Implement error handling and duplicate detection

**4. Event Processing Logic**
processVoteCastEvent() implementation:
```typescript
private async processVoteCastEvent(event: ethers.Log, precomputedArgs?: unknown[]) {
  // Parse event: proposalId, voter, support
  // Check if vote already cached by txHash
  // If exists: log and return (idempotency)
  // If new: upsert VoteCache record using [proposalId, voter] unique constraint
  // Handle vote changes: if voter changes vote, update support, txHash, blockNumber, timestamp, updatedAt
  // Log successful cache update
}
```

**5. Startup Integration**
Update apps/frontend/lib/blockchain-listener.ts or create new startup hook:
- Export startGovernanceListener() function
- Call from same location where startBlockchainListener() is called
- Ensure governance listener starts alongside existing listener

**Architecture Notes:**
- NO signature verification - events are already verified by blockchain
- NO vote submission endpoints - votes happen directly on-chain
- Database is read-only cache for fast queries
- Upsert pattern ensures idempotency and handles vote changes
- Event listener syncs blockchain state to VoteCache table
- Frontend queries VoteCache for vote counts instead of ProposalVote

**Environment Variables:**
Add to .env and .env.example:
```
NEXT_PUBLIC_GOVERNANCE_CONTRACT_ADDRESS=0x...
ETHEREUM_RPC_URL=https://sepolia.infura.io/v3/...
```

tasks.title: Create Finalization Eligibility Checker
tasks.description: Build utility to check if proposal can be finalized based on on-chain vote data
tasks.testStrategy:
Unit tests with mocked contract:
(1) canFinalize=true when voting ended, not finalized, has votes
(2) canFinalize=false when alreadyFinalized=true, reason='Proposal already finalized'
(3) canFinalize=false when votingEnded=false, reason includes time remaining
(4) canFinalize=false when totalVotes=0, reason='No votes cast'
(5) passThresholdMet=true when upvotes >= 2/3 of totalVotes (boundary test: 67 upvotes, 33 downvotes)
(6) passThresholdMet=false when upvotes < 2/3 (test: 66 upvotes, 34 downvotes)
(7) votingEnded=true when currentTime >= votingEndsAt
(8) votingEnded=false when currentTime < votingEndsAt
(9) formatTimeRemaining() returns correct format for various durations
(10) calculatePassPercentage() returns accurate percentage

Integration test:
- Deploy test proposal to Sepolia with 7-day voting period
- Cast 10 upvotes and 4 downvotes on-chain
- Call checkFinalizationEligibility() before voting ends, verify canFinalize=false
- Wait until voting period ends
- Call checkFinalizationEligibility() after voting ends, verify canFinalize=true, passThresholdMet=true
- Call contract.finalizeProposal()
- Call checkFinalizationEligibility() again, verify alreadyFinalized=true, canFinalize=false

tasks.title: Implement Proposal Finalization API Endpoint
tasks.description: Create endpoint to trigger proposal finalization on-chain
tasks.details:
Create apps/frontend/app/api/proposals/[id]/finalize/route.ts. POST endpoint restricted to platform admin or automated service.

The endpoint should:

1. Validate proposal can be finalized:
   - Proposal exists and has onChainId
   - Voting period has ended (check proposal.votingEndsAt)
   - Not already finalized (onChainStatus not FINALIZED, PASSED, or FAILED)

2. Verify quorum from on-chain data:
   - Call contract.getProposal(onChainId) to get vote counts
   - Calculate participation: voterCount / totalUsers
   - Require >= 60% participation for quorum

3. Call simple finalization:
   - NO voter arrays, vote arrays, or signature arrays
   - Simple call: contract.finalizeProposal(onChainId)
   - Contract internally tallies its own stored votes

4. Transaction handling:
   - Initialize provider: new ethers.JsonRpcProvider(process.env.ETHEREUM_RPC_URL)
   - Create wallet: new Wallet(process.env.PLATFORM_SIGNER_PRIVATE_KEY, provider)
   - Create contract instance using createGovernanceContractWithSigner(wallet)
   - Wait for transaction confirmation: await tx.wait()

5. Update database after confirmation:
   - Set proposal.onChainStatus to PASSED or FAILED based on contract event
   - Store finalizationTxHash
   - Update legacy status field for backward compatibility

6. Error handling:
   - Gas estimation failures
   - Transaction reverts
   - Network issues
   - Use try-catch with transaction rollback on failure

Implementation pattern follows apps/frontend/lib/blockchain-listener.ts:1-50 for provider/contract setup.

Environment variables required:
- PLATFORM_SIGNER_PRIVATE_KEY - Private key for platform signer
- ETHEREUM_RPC_URL - RPC endpoint (e.g., Alchemy Sepolia)

Database schema reference: packages/db/prisma/schema.prisma:293-309 (Proposal model)

   ```typescript
   import { AlchemyProvider } from 'ethers';
   
   async function checkBalance() {
     const provider = new AlchemyProvider('sepolia', process.env.ALCHEMY_API_KEY);
     const balance = await provider.getBalance(process.env.PLATFORM_SIGNER_ADDRESS!);
     const ethBalance = Number(balance) / 1e18;
     
     console.log(`Platform wallet balance: ${ethBalance} ETH`);
     
     if (ethBalance < 0.1) {
       console.warn('⚠️  LOW BALANCE ALERT: Please fund wallet!');
       // Send email/Slack alert
     }
   }
   
   checkBalance();
   ```

5. Security measures:
   - Add .env to .gitignore (verify it's there)
   - Restrict server access to private key file
   - Use environment-specific wallets (staging vs production)
   - Set up key rotation policy (every 90 days)
   - Enable 2FA on any cloud services storing keys
   - Consider using AWS KMS or HashiCorp Vault for production

   - Create runbook for wallet funding process
   - Document emergency key rotation procedure

tasks.title: Update Documentation with On-Chain Governance Guide
tasks.description: Create comprehensive documentation for direct on-chain governance feature
tasks.details:
Create docs/governance-transparency.md:

# On-Chain Governance Transparency

## Overview
W3Uptime uses blockchain technology to ensure transparent, verifiable governance. All proposals and votes are recorded directly on the Sepolia Ethereum testnet using a direct on-chain voting model.

## Voting Model: Direct On-Chain

Unlike gasless signature-based systems, W3Uptime uses **direct on-chain voting** where:
- Each vote is a blockchain transaction
- Voters pay their own gas fees (~$0.50-1.00)
- Votes are immediately visible on-chain
- No batch submission or signature aggregation needed
- Full transparency and real-time verification

## For Users

### Creating an On-Chain Proposal
1. Navigate to Community > Submit Proposal
2. Fill in title, description, type, and tags
3. Check "Create on-chain proposal"
4. Click Submit - MetaMask will prompt for transaction signature
5. Pay gas fee (estimated: ~$0.50 on Sepolia)
6. Wait for confirmation (~15 seconds)
7. Proposal is now verifiable on Etherscan

### Voting on Proposals
1. Open any active on-chain proposal
2. Click "Upvote On-Chain" or "Downvote On-Chain"
3. Review gas estimate in confirmation dialog
4. Confirm in MetaMask and pay gas fee (~$0.50-1.00)
5. Your vote is recorded immediately on blockchain
6. View your vote transaction on Etherscan
7. **Note**: Vote changes are NOT allowed after submission

### Verifying Your Vote
1. After voting, your vote is immediately visible on-chain
2. Click "Verify My Vote" on proposal page
3. System fetches your vote directly from blockchain
4. View your VoteCast transaction on Etherscan
5. Verify vote timestamp and block number

### Understanding Governance Rules
- **Voting Period**: 7 days from proposal creation
- **Quorum Requirement**: 50% of users must participate
- **Passing Threshold**: 2/3 (66.67%) of votes must be upvotes
- **Finalization**: Automatic within 24 hours after voting ends
- **Vote Changes**: NOT allowed (each address can vote once)
- **Gas Costs**: Users pay gas for votes, platform pays for finalization

## For Developers

### Smart Contract Details
- **Network**: Sepolia Testnet (Chain ID: 11155111)
- **Contract Address**: [See .env.example]
- **Verified Source**: https://sepolia.etherscan.io/address/...
- **Voting Model**: Direct on-chain (no EIP-712 signatures)

### Architecture
```
User → MetaMask → Smart Contract (vote tx) → Blockchain
                     ↓
              VoteCast Event
                     ↓
           Event Listener → Database (cache)
```

### Key Technologies
- **Direct On-Chain Voting**: Each vote is a blockchain transaction
- **ethers.js v6**: Blockchain interaction library
- **Sepolia**: Ethereum testnet
- **Prisma ORM**: Database management (vote caching)

### API Endpoints
- `POST /api/proposals` - Create proposal (on-chain or off-chain)
- `GET /api/proposals/[id]/verify-vote` - Verify vote on-chain via contract.getVote()
- `GET /api/proposals/dashboard` - Transparency dashboard data
- `GET /api/proposals/export` - Export governance data with VoteCast events

### Database Schema
See IMPLEMENTATION_CHANGES.md for Prisma schema updates.
- SignedVote model removed (no signatures)
- VoteCache model added for on-chain vote caching

### Smart Contract Functions
```solidity
function createProposal(bytes32 contentHash, uint256 votingDuration) external returns (uint256)
function vote(uint256 proposalId, bool support) external
function getVote(uint256 proposalId, address voter) external view returns (bool hasVoted, bool support)
function finalizeProposal(uint256 proposalId) external
function getProposal(uint256 proposalId) external view returns (Proposal)
```

### Running Finalization Scheduler
```bash
# Manual run
npm run finalize-proposals

# Setup cron (Linux/Mac)
crontab -e
*/15 * * * * cd /path/to/w3uptime && npm run finalize-proposals >> logs/finalization.log 2>&1
```

### Testing
```bash
# Smart contract tests
cd contracts && npx hardhat test

# Integration tests
npm run test:integration
```

## For Validators

### Why Direct On-Chain Voting?
- **Maximum Transparency**: All votes immediately visible on blockchain
- **Real-Time Verification**: Anyone can verify votes at any time
- **Simplicity**: No signature aggregation or batch submission complexity
- **Immutability**: Votes cannot be altered once submitted
- **Trust**: No reliance on platform to submit signatures

### Trade-offs
- **Gas Costs**: Users pay gas (~$0.50-1.00 per vote on Sepolia)
- **Finality**: Votes cannot be changed after submission
- **Scalability**: Higher cost than signature-based systems

### Viewing Transparency Dashboard
Visit /community/dashboard to see:
- All on-chain proposals
- Real-time vote counts from blockchain
- Individual voter addresses and transactions
- Etherscan verification links for all votes
- Platform statistics

### Exporting Governance Data
Click "Export Data" on dashboard to download complete JSON export including:
- All proposals and metadata
- VoteCast event data with transaction hashes
- Voter addresses and vote types
- Block numbers and timestamps

## Troubleshooting

### "Please switch to Sepolia network"
1. Open MetaMask
2. Click network dropdown
3. Select "Sepolia" (or add if not listed)

### "Insufficient funds for gas"
- Get Sepolia ETH from faucet: https://sepoliafaucet.com/
- Needed for both creating proposals AND voting

### "Transaction would fail"
- Check voting period hasn't ended
- Verify proposal is in ACTIVE status
- Ensure you haven't already voted
- Try refreshing page

### "Already voted"
- Direct on-chain voting does NOT support vote changes
- Each address can only vote once per proposal
- This ensures vote integrity and prevents manipulation

### Vote not showing as verified
- Votes are verified immediately via contract.getVote()
- Check you're connected with the wallet you voted from
- Verify transaction confirmed on Etherscan
- Contact support if issue persists

## Security Considerations

### For Users
- Never share your private key
- Verify contract address before signing
- Only sign transactions you understand
- Use hardware wallet for large holdings
- Be aware votes are permanent (cannot change)

### For Platform
- Platform wallet private key stored in encrypted .env
- Regular security audits of smart contracts
- Rate limiting on API endpoints
- Monitoring for unusual activity
- Only platform pays finalization gas, users pay vote gas

## Comparison: Direct vs Gasless Voting

| Feature | Direct On-Chain | Gasless (EIP-712) |
|---------|----------------|-------------------|
| User Gas Cost | ~$0.50-1.00 per vote | $0 (free) |
| Vote Visibility | Immediate | After batch submission |
| Vote Changes | Not allowed | Allowed until finalization |
| Complexity | Simple | Signature aggregation |
| Trust Model | Trustless | Trust platform for batch |
| Verification | Real-time | After finalization |

W3Uptime uses **Direct On-Chain** for maximum transparency.

## Future Enhancements
- Mainnet deployment
- Delegation support
- Proposal deposit requirements

tasks.title: Implement Nonce-Based Duplicate Vote Prevention in W3Governance Contract
tasks.description: Replace boolean vote tracking with nonce-based system that tracks vote count per proposal per voter, enabling gas-efficient O(1) duplicate detection while maintaining voter history.
tasks.testStrategy:
Manual testing in Remix IDE on Sepolia testnet following Task 3 and Task 4 deployment workflow:

**1. Pre-Testing Setup:**
- Ensure Task 3 (W3Governance contract with nonce-based voting) is implemented
- Ensure Task 4 (deployment script) has deployed the updated contract to Sepolia
- Ensure Task 34 (claimReputation) is functional and test account has reputation balance
- Confirm test wallet has sufficient Sepolia ETH for gas fees
- Note the deployed contract address from Task 4

**2. Initial State Verification:**
- In Remix IDE, load deployed W3Governance contract at verified address
- Call `voteNonces(proposalId, voterAddress)` for test proposal - expect 0 (not voted)
- Call `getVote(proposalId, voterAddress)` - expect 0 (not voted)
- Call `hasVoted(proposalId, voterAddress)` - expect false

**3. First Vote Test (Upvote):**
- Create test proposal using createProposal() or use existing proposal ID
- Check initial reputation: call `reputationBalance(msg.sender)` - expect >= 100
- Call `vote(proposalId, true)` to cast upvote
- Transaction should succeed and emit VoteCast event
- Verify event parameters: proposalId, voter address, support=true, nonce=1, timestamp
- Call `voteNonces(proposalId, voterAddress)` - expect 1
- Call `getVote(proposalId, voterAddress)` - expect 1 (upvote)
- Call `hasVoted(proposalId, voterAddress)` - expect true
- Verify reputation deducted: `reputationBalance(msg.sender)` should be reduced by 100

**4. Duplicate Vote Prevention Test:**
- Attempt to call `vote(proposalId, true)` again with same account
- Transaction should REVERT with "Already voted on this proposal" or AlreadyVoted error
- Verify `voteNonces(proposalId, voterAddress)` remains 1 (unchanged)
- Verify proposal vote counts unchanged (no double counting)

**5. Different Vote Type Test:**
- Attempt to call `vote(proposalId, false)` with same account (trying to change vote)
- Transaction should REVERT with duplicate vote error
- Confirm nonce-based check prevents vote type changes
- Verify vote type remains upvote: `getVote(proposalId, voterAddress)` returns 1

**6. Cross-Account Voting Test:**
- Switch to different MetaMask account in Remix
- Ensure second account has reputation balance via claimReputation()
- Call `vote(proposalId, false)` with second account to cast downvote
- Transaction should succeed
- Verify Account 1: `voteNonces(proposalId, account1)` = 1, `getVote()` = 1 (upvote)
- Verify Account 2: `voteNonces(proposalId, account2)` = 1, `getVote()` = 2 (downvote)
- Verify proposal counters: upvotes=1, downvotes=1

**7. Multi-Proposal Voting Test:**
- Create second proposal or use different proposalId
- Call `vote(secondProposalId, true)` with Account 1
- Should succeed (nonce is per-proposal-per-voter)
- Verify: `voteNonces(secondProposalId, account1)` = 1
- Verify: `voteNonces(firstProposalId, account1)` still = 1 (unchanged)
- Confirm isolation: voting on proposal 2 doesn't affect proposal 1 nonce

**8. Gas Efficiency Verification:**
- Compare gas costs in Remix transaction logs:
  - First vote transaction gas cost
  - Duplicate vote revert gas cost (should be minimal due to early revert)
- Verify duplicate check happens before expensive operations
- Confirm single storage slot read for nonce check (O(1) complexity)

**9. Edge Case: Zero Reputation Test:**
- Use account with 0 reputation balance
- Attempt `vote(proposalId, true)`
- Should revert with "Insufficient reputation" BEFORE nonce check
- Verify nonce remains 0: `voteNonces(proposalId, zeroRepAccount)` = 0

**10. Integration with Existing Functions:**
- Call `getProposal(proposalId)` to verify vote counts match nonce-tracked voters
- Verify compatibility: all existing Task 3 functions still work correctly
- Test finalization flow (Task 13) works with nonce-based vote tracking

**Success Criteria:**
- All nonce checks prevent duplicate voting with O(1) gas cost
- getVote() returns correct vote types (1=upvote, 2=downvote, 0=not voted)
- hasVoted() accurately reflects voting status based on nonces
- Vote counts accurately increment without double counting
- Reputation deductions work correctly per vote
- Cross-proposal voting isolation confirmed
- No regression in existing contract functionality

tasks.title: Extend Prisma User Model for Reputation Claiming Tracking
tasks.description: Add tracking fields to User model for claimed vs unclaimed reputation (totalReputation, claimedReputation, lastClaimAt) with migration, and update validation processing to increment totalReputation instead of reputationScore.
tasks.details:
## Schema Changes

Edit `packages/db/prisma/schema.prisma` (packages/db/prisma/schema.prisma:10-38). Add new fields to User model after line 37 (after `reputationScore`):

```prisma
model User {
  // ... existing fields ...
  reputationScore     Int                @default(0)
  
  // On-chain reputation claiming fields
  totalReputation     Int                @default(0)  // Total earned from validations
  claimedReputation   Int                @default(0)  // Transferred to on-chain balance
  lastClaimAt         DateTime?                       // Last claim timestamp
}
```

**Migration Command:**
Run in `packages/db` directory:
```bash
npx prisma migrate dev --name add-reputation-claiming-fields
```

## Update Reputation Earning Logic

Modify `apps/hub/src/services/reputation.ts` (apps/hub/src/services/reputation.ts:22-56):

**In `applyGoodTick()` function (line 22):**
```typescript
export async function applyGoodTick(publicKey: string) {
  const user = await prisma.user.findUnique({
    where: { publicKey },
    select: { id: true, goodTicks: true, badTicks: true, totalReputation: true },
  });
  if (!user) return;

  const goodTicks = user.goodTicks + 1;
  const badTicks = user.badTicks;
  const totalReputation = user.totalReputation + 1;  // Increment unclaimed reputation

  const reputationScore = computeReputationScore({ goodTicks, badTicks });

  await prisma.user.update({
    where: { id: user.id },
    data: { goodTicks, reputationScore, totalReputation },
  });
}
```

**Note:** `applyBadTick()` should NOT modify `totalReputation` - bad ticks only affect `reputationScore` calculation but don't reduce earned reputation.

## Backward Compatibility Strategy

1. **Existing Data Safety:**
   - All new fields are NULLABLE or have DEFAULT values
   - `totalReputation` defaults to 0 (users start fresh from implementation date)
   - `claimedReputation` defaults to 0 (no claims made yet)
   - `lastClaimAt` is nullable (users haven't claimed yet)

2. **Existing Logic Preservation:**
   - `reputationScore` calculation unchanged (still uses `goodTicks - BAD_TICK_PENALTY * badTicks`)
   - Existing reputation gates in `apps/frontend/app/api/proposals/ReputationGuard.ts` continue working
   - Frontend display in `apps/frontend/app/(user)/community/CommunityGovernanceClient.tsx` unaffected

3. **Migration Process:**
   - Database migration auto-applies defaults to existing users
   - No manual data backfill required
   - Users accumulate `totalReputation` from migration date forward
   - Existing `reputationScore` values remain intact

## Key Distinctions

| Field | Purpose | Modified By |
|-------|---------|-------------|
| `reputationScore` | Off-chain governance access (existing) | goodTicks/badTicks formula |
| `totalReputation` | Earned reputation available to claim | applyGoodTick() only |
| `claimedReputation` | Amount transferred to blockchain | claimReputation() API (Task 34) |

**Unclaimed Balance Formula:**
```typescript
unclaimedReputation = totalReputation - claimedReputation
```
tasks.testStrategy:
## 1. Migration Verification
Run migration in `packages/db`:
```bash
npx prisma migrate dev --name add-reputation-claiming-fields
npx prisma migrate status
```
Verify migration creates three new User table columns:
- `totalReputation` INT NOT NULL DEFAULT 0
- `claimedReputation` INT NOT NULL DEFAULT 0  
- `lastClaimAt` TIMESTAMP NULL

Check existing user records have default values:
```sql
SELECT id, totalReputation, claimedReputation, lastClaimAt FROM "User" LIMIT 5;
```

## 2. Reputation Earning Logic Test
Start hub server (`npm run dev` in apps/hub) and validator client. Perform monitoring tasks:

**Test A: Good Tick Increments totalReputation**
1. Before validation: Query user `totalReputation` and `goodTicks`
2. Submit valid signed monitoring result via WebSocket
3. Verify database updates:
   - `goodTicks` increased by 1
   - `totalReputation` increased by 1
   - `reputationScore` recalculated correctly

**Test B: Bad Tick Does NOT Affect totalReputation**
1. Submit invalid signature validation
2. Verify: `badTicks` increases, `totalReputation` unchanged
3. Verify: `reputationScore` decreases (existing formula)

## 3. Backward Compatibility Verification
**Existing User Data:**
- Verify existing users have `totalReputation = 0`, `claimedReputation = 0`, `lastClaimAt = null`
- Confirm existing `reputationScore` values unchanged

**Existing Governance Features:**
- Test creating proposal with existing reputation threshold (MIN_REP_FOR_PROPOSAL = 200)
- Test voting on proposals with existing reputation check (MIN_REP_FOR_VOTE = 100)
- Verify `apps/frontend/hooks/useReputation.ts` still functions correctly
- Confirm community governance page displays reputation without errors

## 4. Edge Cases
- User with 0 validations: `totalReputation = 0`
- Multiple rapid good ticks: totalReputation increments atomically
- Database rollback: migration can be reverted cleanly

tasks.title: Update Hub Validator Processing to Increment Reputation for Successful Uptime Checks
tasks.description: Modify hub's validator processing to award reputation points when validators perform successful uptime checks (monitor tick validations), using existing signature validation infrastructure and ensuring atomic database updates.
tasks.testStrategy:
**Manual Testing:**

1. **Start the Hub Server:**
   - Run `npm run dev --filter=hub`
   - Verify server starts without errors
   - Check console for "WebSocket server is running on port 8080"

2. **Connect a Validator:**
   - Run a validator client: `npm run dev --filter=validator`
   - Verify signup message is processed
   - Check hub logs for "Client connected"

3. **Trigger Uptime Checks:**
   - Wait for monitor distribution cycle (30 seconds)
   - Observe console logs for validation messages
   - Verify reputation log messages appear: "Reputation awarded: User [id] earned 1 points for uptime check"

4. **Verify Database Updates:**
   - Use pgAdmin or Prisma Studio to check User table
   - Query a validator's record: `SELECT id, publicKey, goodTicks, badTicks, reputationScore FROM "User" WHERE "publicKey" = '[validator-public-key]';`
   - Expected behavior:
     - After successful signature + successful uptime check: goodTicks +1, reputationScore +2 (1 for signature + 1 for uptime)
     - After successful signature + failed uptime check: goodTicks +1, reputationScore +0 (1 for signature - 1 for failed uptime)
     - After failed signature: badTicks +1, reputationScore -2 (existing penalty)

5. **Test GOOD Status Uptime Checks:**
   - Configure monitor with URL that returns 200 OK
   - Verify validator receives validation request
   - Verify "GOOD" status in callback
   - Check console for: "Reputation awarded: User [id] earned 1 points for uptime check"
   - Verify reputationScore increased in database

6. **Test BAD Status Uptime Checks:**
   - Configure monitor with URL that returns 500 or times out
   - Verify validator receives validation request
   - Verify "BAD" status in callback
   - Check console for: "Reputation penalty: User [id] lost 1 points for failed uptime check"
   - Verify reputationScore decreased (but not below 0) in database

7. **Test Race Condition Handling:**
   - Add multiple monitors (3-5) to trigger concurrent validations
   - Trigger multiple rapid-fire validations from same validator
   - Verify final reputationScore matches expected calculation
   - No database errors in logs
   - All updates successfully applied

8. **Test Edge Cases:**
   - Missing publicKey: Verify warning log appears without crashing
   - Non-existent user: Verify warning log appears without crashing
   - Validator disconnects mid-validation: Verify cleanup works correctly

**Integration Testing:**

1. **Full Workflow Test:**
   - Start hub, create monitor, connect validator
   - Let system run for 5 minutes (10 validation cycles)
   - Calculate expected reputation:
     - Per cycle: 1 signature validation + 1 uptime check (if GOOD)
     - Expected increase: 2 points per successful cycle
   - Verify actual reputationScore matches expected value ±2 points

2. **Log Verification:**
   - Review hub logs for all reputation award messages
   - Count awards vs penalties
   - Verify count matches number of validations
   - Ensure no error stack traces appear

3. **Reputation Transparency:**
   - Export user reputation data via API: GET /api/validator/dashboard
   - Verify reputationScore is accurate
   - Cross-reference with database query
   - Verify frontend displays updated reputation

**Expected Outcomes:**

- No TypeScript compilation errors
- No runtime errors in hub logs
- Reputation increases for successful uptime checks
- Reputation decreases for failed uptime checks (but never below 0)
- All database updates are atomic and consistent
- Transparent logging of all reputation changes

tasks.title: Optimize Gas Usage in W3Governance Contract Finalization Function
tasks.description: Review and optimize the finalizeProposal() function in W3Governance.sol to remove O(n²) duplicate voter checks if present, ensure nonce-based duplicate prevention is functioning, cache array lengths in loops, and minimize reputation check gas overhead.
tasks.testStrategy:
**Gas Cost Measurement and Testing:**

1. **Baseline Gas Measurement (Before Optimization):**
   - Deploy current W3Governance contract to Sepolia testnet
   - Create test proposal via createProposal()
   - Call finalizeProposal() with varying voter counts: 10, 50, 100, 500 addresses
   - Record gas used from Etherscan transaction details for each test case
   - Document baseline costs in spreadsheet or test notes

2. **Post-Optimization Gas Measurement:**
   - Deploy optimized W3Governance contract to Sepolia testnet (new deployment address)
   - Repeat identical finalization tests with same voter counts
   - Record optimized gas costs from Etherscan
   - Calculate gas savings: `(baseline - optimized) / baseline * 100%`
   - Target: Minimum 20-30% gas reduction for 100+ voter scenarios

3. **Functional Correctness Tests:**
   - Verify finalizeProposal() still correctly counts upvotes/downvotes
   - Test with all voters supporting (100% upvotes)
   - Test with all voters opposing (100% downvotes)
   - Test with mixed votes (60% upvote, 40% downvote)
   - Confirm ProposalFinalized event emits correct values
   - Verify proposal.finalized flag is set to true
   - Verify proposal.passed is true when upvotes > downvotes

4. **Nonce-Based Duplicate Prevention Validation:**
   - Attempt to submit same voter address twice with same nonce (should fail)
   - Verify usedNonces mapping prevents duplicate vote processing
   - Confirm backend filtering is primary defense, nonce is secondary safety net
   - Test edge case: Same voter with different nonce (should be prevented by backend, but nonce prevents on-chain)

5. **Reputation Check Validation:**
   - Finalize proposal with mix of voters having reputation (>0) and no reputation (0)
   - Verify voters with reputation get weighted vote (2x) correctly
   - Verify voters without reputation get standard vote (1x) correctly
   - Confirm total upvotes/downvotes match expected weighted sum
   - Check gas costs for reputation lookups are minimal (<5% of total)

6. **Edge Case Testing:**
   - Finalize with 0 voters (empty arrays)
   - Finalize with single voter
   - Finalize with maximum expected voters (1000+, if feasible on testnet)
   - Verify no out-of-gas errors with large voter sets

7. **Comparison Documentation:**
   - Create comparison table: Voter Count | Before Gas | After Gas | Savings %
   - Document specific optimizations and their individual impact
   - Verify total gas savings justify code changes
   - Etherscan transaction links for before/after evidence

tasks.title: Create Comprehensive Integration Tests for Reputation System
tasks.description: Build end-to-end integration tests for the reputation system covering earning, claiming, governance operations, database/blockchain sync, error cases, and gas cost analysis on Sepolia testnet
tasks.testStrategy:
**Pre-Test Setup:**
1. Fund test wallet with 0.5 Sepolia ETH from faucet
2. Deploy ReputationSystem smart contract to Sepolia (if not already deployed)
3. Set all environment variables in .env.test

**Execution:**

2. Run `npm run test:integration` to execute all tests
3. Each test should pass with green status
4. Gas cost report generated in gas-costs-report.json

**Verification Checklist:**
1. **Earning Reputation**: User's `goodTicks` and `reputationScore` increment correctly in database after calling `applyGoodTick()`
2. **Claiming Reputation**: Transaction succeeds on Sepolia, `balanceOf()` returns correct amount, database updates `onChainReputation`
3. **Creating Proposal**: Proposal created in database, reputation deducted by 500, MIN_REP_FOR_PROPOSAL enforced
4. **Voting**: Vote recorded in ProposalVote table, reputation deducted by 100, MIN_REP_FOR_VOTE enforced
5. **State Consistency**: On-chain balance matches database `onChainReputation`, available reputation = earned - claimed - spent
6. **Insufficient Reputation**: API returns 403 status code with appropriate error message
7. **Double Claiming**: Second transaction with same nonce reverts with `NonceAlreadyUsed` error

- Check for orphaned records or inconsistent states
- Verify foreign key relationships intact

- Verify backend API logs for signature generation errors
- Check database constraints and migrations applied correctly
- Ensure test wallet has sufficient ETH for gas fees

tasks.title: Create Comprehensive Reputation System Documentation
tasks.description: Develop complete documentation covering the reputation system architecture, claiming mechanisms, thresholds, and user guide with API reference for the W3Uptime governance system.
tasks.details:
Create comprehensive documentation in docs/reputation-system.md covering:

## 1. Reputation System Overview Section
- Explain the dual-source reputation model (validator work + customer activity)
- Document the off-chain scoring system that tracks work before on-chain claims
- Explain the lazy claiming pattern (users must manually claim via blockchain transaction)
- Describe gas efficiency benefits of lazy claiming vs. real-time on-chain updates

## 2. How Reputation is Earned

**Validator Activity (apps/hub/src/services/reputation.ts:22-56):**
- Document goodTicks: awarded for successful signature validations and uptime checks (reference Task #44)
- Document badTicks: penalties for failed validations (BAD_TICK_PENALTY = 2)
- Formula: `validatorScore = goodTicks - (2 * badTicks)`
- POINTS_PER_REP conversion (100 raw points = 1 reputation score)

**Customer Activity (apps/frontend/app/api/proposals/ReputationGuard.ts:10-105):**
- Monitor creation: +20 points per active monitor
- Deposit amounts: tiered scoring based on ETH deposited (0.1, 1, 5 ETH thresholds)
- Account age: +5 points per day since account creation
- Total formula: `totalScore = validatorScore + monitorScore + depositScore + ageScore`

## 3. Reputation Requirements and Thresholds

Reference apps/frontend/app/api/proposals/ReputationGuard.ts:5-7:
- **Create Proposal**: MIN_REP_FOR_PROPOSAL = 200 points
- **Comment on Proposal**: MIN_REP_FOR_COMMENT = 100 points  
- **Vote on Proposal**: MIN_REP_FOR_VOTE = 50 points

Explain on-chain enforcement via W3Governance smart contract (reference Task #34).

## 4. Lazy Claiming Pattern Architecture

**Off-Chain Tracking:**
- Database schema: User.goodTicks, User.badTicks, User.reputationScore (packages/db/prisma/schema.prisma:34-37)
- Real-time updates in hub WebSocket server (apps/hub/src/services/reputation.ts)
- API endpoint for checking unclaimed reputation: GET /api/community/reputation (apps/frontend/app/api/community/reputation/route.ts)

**On-Chain Claiming (reference Task #34):**
- Contract function: W3Governance.claimReputation(uint256 amount, bytes signature)
- Platform signature validates off-chain reputation
- Gas costs: estimated ~50,000-80,000 gas (~$2-5 on mainnet)
- Benefits: users only pay gas when needed for governance actions

## 5. Gas Efficiency Benefits
- Compare lazy claiming (one transaction when needed) vs. real-time updates (transaction per validation)
- Example calculation: 1000 validations = 1 claim transaction vs. 1000 transactions
- Cost savings: ~$2 vs ~$2000-5000 (approximate, depends on gas prices)
- User control: claim only when reaching governance thresholds

## 6. User Guide with Claim Flow

**Step-by-Step Claiming Process:**
1. Connect wallet to W3Uptime platform
2. Navigate to Community/Governance section
3. View current off-chain reputation balance
4. Click "Claim Reputation On-Chain" button
5. MetaMask popup shows gas estimate
6. Confirm transaction with platform signature verification
7. Wait for blockchain confirmation
8. On-chain balance updated, ready for governance actions

**Screenshots to Include:**
- Reputation dashboard showing unclaimed balance
- Claim button with gas estimate preview
- MetaMask confirmation dialog
- Success confirmation with Etherscan link
- Governance proposal creation showing on-chain balance

## 7. API Documentation

**GET /api/community/reputation**
- Authentication: Required (wallet signature)
- Response: ReputationBreakdown interface with totalScore, validatorScore, customerScore breakdown
- Example response:
```json
{
  "success": true,
  "data": {
    "totalScore": 450,
    "validatorScore": 280,
    "customerScore": 170,
    "monitorScore": 100,
    "depositScore": 2,
    "ageScore": 68,
    "thresholds": {
      "createProposal": 200,
      "comment": 100,
      "vote": 50
    }
  }
}
```

**POST /api/reputation/claim** (to be implemented, reference Task #34)
- Request body: { amount: number }
- Returns platform signature for contract interaction
- Frontend uses signature with W3Governance.claimReputation()

## 8. Update CLAUDE.md

Add new section "## Reputation System Architecture":
- Reference reputation.ts service location and formula
- Document ReputationGuard.ts helper functions
- Explain lazy claiming pattern for future developers
- Link to full docs/reputation-system.md documentation
- Note: Reputation affects governance participation, not core monitoring functionality

## Technical Implementation Notes
- Reputation calculation is deterministic based on database state
- No caching layer currently (computed on each API call)
- Signature generation for claims uses ethers.js ECDSA (reference UserTransactions.sol pattern)
- Platform signer private key stored in environment variable PLATFORM_SIGNER_PRIVATE_KEY
